bytes.topic: Testing Strategy — unit vs integration vs E2E pyramid, what to test at each level
bytes.title: Jest Testing Pyramid — What to Test at Each Layer
bytes.body: Jest handles all three layers of the testing pyramid, but mixing responsibilities kills maintainability. Unit tests belong in `.test.ts` files colocated with source — isolate pure functions and hooks using `jest.fn()` and `jest.spyOn()`, mock everything external with `jest.mock('./api')`. They run in milliseconds and should dominate your suite (70%+). Integration tests wire multiple units together without mocking internals — test a Redux slice with its reducer and selectors, or a React component tree using `@testing-library/react` with a real store but a mocked HTTP layer via `jest.mock` or `msw`. These catch contract mismatches between units. E2E belongs in Playwright or Cypress, not Jest — if you're fighting jsdom limitations for a 'full flow' test, that's a signal you've misplaced it. Use `jest.config.ts` projects to enforce separation: define `unit` and `integration` projects with distinct `testMatch` globs and `setupFilesAfterFramework` so each layer gets its own bootstrap. Never let a unit test import from another feature's module without mocking it.

bytes.topic: Test Behaviour Not Implementation — Testing Library philosophy, query by role not by class
bytes.title: Jest + Testing Library — Query by Role, Not by Class
bytes.body:
Most Jest test suites break silently when developers rename a CSS class or restructure markup. That's because they're testing implementation, not behaviour. Testing Library's philosophy flips this: query what the user perceives, not what the DOM looks like internally.

Prefer `getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })` over `querySelector('.btn-primary')` or `getByTestId('submit-btn')`. Roles map to ARIA semantics — a `<button>` is always 'button', an `<input type="checkbox">` is always 'checkbox'. Your test survives refactors as long as the user-facing contract holds.

Query priority (highest to lowest): `getByRole` → `getByLabelText` → `getByPlaceholderText` → `getByText` → `getByDisplayValue` → `getByAltText` → `getByTitle` → `getByTestId`. Reach for `getByTestId` only when semantic alternatives are genuinely unavailable.

Pair this with `userEvent` over `fireEvent` — `userEvent.click()` dispatches the full browser event sequence (pointerdown, mousedown, focus, click), catching bugs that raw `fireEvent.click()` masks. With Jest's jsdom environment, this distinction matters because jsdom simulates, not renders.

bytes.topic: Component Testing — rendering, user interaction, asserting on visible output
bytes.title: Jest + React Testing Library — Testing Components Without Implementation Details
bytes.body:
Jest alone doesn't render React components — it needs a DOM environment. With `@testing-library/react`, you render into a `jsdom` environment that Jest provides, then query and interact via user-facing selectors.

`render()` mounts the component. Query functions like `getByRole`, `getByText`, and `findByLabelText` target elements the way a user would — not by class names or test IDs. `getBy*` throws immediately if nothing matches; `findBy*` returns a Promise and retries until a timeout, essential for async state updates.

For interactions, use `userEvent` (v14+) over `fireEvent` — it simulates real browser behavior including focus, pointer events, and keyboard sequences. Always `await userEvent.setup()` before dispatching events.

Assertions land on `expect()` with jest-dom matchers: `toBeVisible()`, `toBeDisabled()`, `toHaveValue()`. These assertions inspect the rendered DOM, not component state — keeping tests resilient to refactors.

Wrap any render that triggers `setState` inside `act()`, or let RTL handle it automatically via `userEvent` and `findBy*` queries.

bytes.topic: Unit Testing — pure functions, custom hooks with renderHook
bytes.title: Jest — Unit Testing Pure Functions and Custom Hooks with renderHook
bytes.body:
Pure functions are Jest's easiest target: no mocks, no setup — just input/output assertions. Use `test.each` to drive multiple cases without repetition, keeping your suite DRY and exhaustive.

Custom hooks are trickier because they rely on React's runtime. Jest alone can't call a hook directly — you need `renderHook` from `@testing-library/react`. It mounts the hook in a minimal React tree, giving you back `result.current` to inspect state and `act()` to flush updates.

For async hooks (data fetching, debounce), wrap state-changing calls in `act()` — Jest will warn loudly if you forget. Use `waitFor` when the update is asynchronous and you need to poll until an assertion passes.

Pair `renderHook` with `jest.useFakeTimers()` for hooks that use `setTimeout` or `setInterval` — advance time with `jest.advanceTimersByTime(ms)` and assert mid-flight state. Always call `jest.useRealTimers()` in `afterEach` to avoid test bleed.

bytes.topic: Integration Testing — testing multiple components together, mocking at the right boundary
bytes.title: Jest Integration Testing — Mock at the Boundary, Not the Internals
bytes.body:
Integration tests in Jest verify that multiple components wire together correctly. The key discipline: mock external boundaries (APIs, localStorage, third-party modules), never internal collaborators. If you mock a child component inside a parent integration test, you're just writing a unit test with extra steps.

Use `jest.mock()` at the module level to stub network calls via `msw` or by mocking `fetch` directly. Prefer `@testing-library/react` with `render` to mount the full component tree, then assert on DOM output — not implementation details. `waitFor` and `findBy*` queries handle async state updates cleanly.

A common mistake: mocking `useCartStore` or a context provider when testing a checkout flow. Instead, wrap the tree in the real provider with seeded state. Mock only the `POST /orders` fetch at the network layer. This catches wiring bugs — wrong prop drilling, missing context, broken event chains — that pure unit tests miss entirely. Keep `beforeEach` resets with `jest.clearAllMocks()` to prevent cross-test bleed.

bytes.topic: E2E Testing — full user flows, critical paths only (auth, onboarding, core feature)
bytes.title: Jest E2E with jest-puppeteer — Testing Critical Auth Flows Without Flakiness
bytes.body:
E2E tests in Jest run through `jest-puppeteer`, which exposes a global `page` (Puppeteer's Page object) and a `browser` instance to every test file. The preset handles browser lifecycle — you never call `puppeteer.launch()` yourself.

The rule for critical-path E2E is ruthless scope: test auth, onboarding, and the one action that is the core of your product. Everything else belongs in unit or integration tests. Flaky E2E suites die because they test too much.

Use `beforeAll` (not `beforeEach`) for navigation when tests in a suite share the same starting URL — saves one full page load per test. Use `waitForNavigation({ waitUntil: 'networkidle0' })` after form submits instead of arbitrary `page.waitForTimeout()` delays; it ties the assertion to actual network quiescence rather than a guess.

For auth specifically, avoid seeding test users through the UI. Use a `globalSetup` script in `jest.config.js` to hit your API directly and create the fixture user once before the suite runs. This keeps the E2E test focused on the browser flow, not setup noise.

bytes.topic: Mocking — MSW for API mocking, when to mock vs use real dependencies
bytes.title: Jest + MSW — API Mocking That Survives Refactors
bytes.body:
`jest.mock('axios')` couples tests to implementation details — swap axios for fetch and every mock breaks. MSW intercepts at the network layer in Jest's Node environment via `msw/node`, making mocks transport-agnostic.

The rule: use MSW when testing code that makes HTTP calls. Use `jest.fn()` for pure business logic, event handlers, or module-level side effects.

Set up a shared server in a setup file referenced by `setupFilesAfterFramework` in `jest.config.ts`, then override per-test with `server.use()` for error paths — 401s, 500s, malformed payloads. Call `server.resetHandlers()` in `afterEach` so per-test overrides don't bleed.

`onUnhandledRequest: 'error'` is your safety net: any unmocked endpoint throws, keeping your test surface explicit. Without it, requests silently fall through in Jest's jsdom or node env — or worse, hit a real service in CI.

Avoid `jest.mock('./api')` for integration-style component tests — you're asserting against a lie. MSW keeps the full fetch path real while controlling only the response, so refactoring the HTTP layer doesn't invalidate your tests.

bytes.topic: Async Testing — waitFor, findBy queries, handling promises and fake timers
bytes.title: Jest Async Testing — waitFor, findBy, and Fake Timers Done Right
bytes.body:
Jest's async utilities each serve a distinct role. `findBy*` queries (from Testing Library) internally call `waitFor`, polling until the element appears or timing out after 1000ms by default — use them for DOM assertions after async state updates. `waitFor` is for non-element assertions: expect a callback to stop throwing within the timeout window.

Avoid wrapping `findBy` inside `waitFor` — it's redundant and masks real errors. Also avoid `act()` wrapping around `waitFor`; Testing Library handles that internally.

For promise-based code, `resolves`/`rejects` matchers keep tests readable: `await expect(fetchUser(1)).resolves.toEqual({ id: 1 })`. Always `await` these — a missing `await` silently passes even when the promise rejects.

Fake timers need special care with async code. When using `jest.useFakeTimers()`, promises may stall because microtasks are queued behind fake macrotasks. Call `jest.runAllTimersAsync()` (Jest 29+) or flush promises explicitly with `await Promise.resolve()` between `jest.advanceTimersByTime()` calls. Always restore with `jest.useRealTimers()` in `afterEach` to prevent timer leaks across tests.

bytes.topic: Snapshot Testing — when it helps vs when it becomes noise
bytes.title: Jest Snapshot Testing — When It Helps vs When It Becomes Noise
bytes.body:
Jest snapshots shine for stable, pure output: serialized data structures, utility function return values, or small UI components where unintended changes matter. Call `expect(tree).toMatchSnapshot()` once, commit the `.snap` file, and Jest catches regressions automatically.

The noise creeps in when snapshots wrap large component trees. Every prop rename, className tweak, or third-party library bump fails the snapshot — forcing mindless `jest --updateSnapshot` runs that defeat the purpose. Developers stop reading diffs and just update blindly.

Prefer `toMatchInlineSnapshot()` for small, stable outputs — the snapshot lives in the test file, so reviewers see it in the PR diff and can reason about it. For component testing, snapshot only the critical subtree using `react-test-renderer` with `toJSON()`, not the entire DOM.

Delete snapshots for components under active churn. Use `--ci` flag in pipelines to block any automatic snapshot creation, forcing explicit intent. Reserve full-tree snapshots for generated code, serialized configs, or CLI output — places where byte-level accuracy is the actual contract.

bytes.topic: Visual Regression — Chromatic with Storybook, screenshot diffing in CI
bytes.title: Jest + Chromatic — Visual Regression Testing for Storybook Stories in CI
bytes.body:
Chromatic wraps Storybook's build output and diffs pixel-by-pixel screenshots across commits — but the test runner that actually executes your stories uses Jest under the hood via `@storybook/test-runner` (built on jest-circus). This means your `play()` functions, `expect()` assertions, and `userEvent` interactions all run through Jest before Chromatic captures the final rendered frame.

Configure `test-runner.ts` at the Storybook root to hook into the Jest lifecycle: use `postVisit` to await animations or fonts before Chromatic snapshots. In CI, run `yarn test-storybook` (Jest) first to catch interaction failures fast and cheap, then run `chromatic --exit-zero-on-changes` to upload the visual build. Gate merges on Chromatic's UI review, not the Jest run — Jest catches broken logic, Chromatic catches broken pixels.

For local diffing without Chromatic, swap in `jest-image-snapshot` via `expect.extend({ toMatchImageSnapshot })` — same Jest `expect` API, but serializes PNG baselines to `__image_snapshots__/`. Delete stale baselines with `--updateSnapshot`, mirroring Jest's snapshot update flow.

bytes.topic: Accessibility Testing — axe-core integration, automated a11y checks in tests
bytes.title: Jest + axe-core — Automated Accessibility Checks in Every Test
bytes.body:
jest-axe wraps axe-core and plugs directly into Jest's `expect` API, letting you catch a11y violations as part of your existing test suite — no browser required. Install `jest-axe` and call `toHaveNoViolations()` after rendering with any DOM-producing utility.

The key is `axe()` which accepts a DOM node or HTML string and returns a results object. Pair it with `@testing-library/react`'s `render` for a tight loop: render → query container → run axe → assert. Jest's `expect.extend(toHaveNoViolations)` registers the custom matcher globally — put this in your `setupFilesAfterFramework` so you don't repeat it per file.

axe-core checks 50+ rules by default: missing `alt`, insufficient color contrast, invalid ARIA roles, missing form labels. You can scope rules via the second argument to `axe()` — pass `{ rules: { 'color-contrast': { enabled: false } } }` to skip rules that don't apply or require visual context. Run the full suite in CI; failures surface as readable Jest diff output listing exact WCAG violation codes.

bytes.topic: Test Data Management — factories, fixtures, seeding test state
bytes.title: Jest Test Data Management — Factories, Fixtures, and Seeding State Without the Mess
bytes.body:
Hardcoding test objects inline is the fastest way to create brittle tests. Jest has no built-in factory system, but you can build a lightweight one using plain functions with `Object.assign` or spread overrides — no library needed.

A factory function returns a complete, valid object with sensible defaults. Call it with partial overrides per test. Pair this with `beforeEach` to seed fresh state and `afterEach` to tear it down, preventing cross-test contamination that `jest.clearAllMocks()` alone won't catch.

For fixtures — static JSON snapshots of API responses — store them in `__fixtures__/` and import directly. Jest's module resolver handles this without any config. Use `jest.mock()` to swap a real module for a fixture-returning mock at the module boundary, not inside the test body.

When seeding async state (e.g. a Redux store or React Query cache), wrap setup in `beforeEach` with `await` and reset via `queryClient.clear()` or `store.dispatch(resetAction())`. Never share mutable state across `describe` blocks — Jest runs them in the same process and isolation is your responsibility.

bytes.topic: CI Integration — parallelizing tests, caching, fail-fast strategies
bytes.title: Jest CI Integration — Parallelizing Tests, Caching, and Fail-Fast Strategies
bytes.body:
Jest's `--runInBand` is a CI killer — drop it unless you're debugging. By default, Jest spawns worker processes equal to available CPUs minus one. On CI, explicitly set `--maxWorkers=50%` to avoid memory exhaustion on shared runners, or use `--maxWorkers=4` for predictable parallelism on known hardware.

Enable the transform cache by persisting `jest-cache` between runs. Point Jest at it with `--cacheDirectory=.jest-cache` and cache that directory in your CI config. This skips Babel/SWC transforms on unchanged files — a 40–60% speedup on warm runs.

For fail-fast behavior, `--bail=1` halts the entire suite on the first test failure, saving runner minutes on obviously broken branches. Pair it with `--testPathPattern` to scope runs to changed files using `git diff`.

Shard large suites across parallel CI jobs using `--shard=1/3`, `--shard=2/3`, `--shard=3/3`. Jest splits by file count, not duration — combine with `--testSequencer` pointing to a custom class that orders files by historical runtime for balanced shards.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/test.yml (relevant jobs section)
jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        shard: [1, 2, 3]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: .jest-cache
          key: jest-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
      - run: |
          npx jest \
            --shard=${{ matrix.shard }}/3 \
            --maxWorkers=50% \
            --cacheDirectory=.jest-cache \
            --bail=1 \
            --ci

bytes.topic: Vitest-specific — vite-native speed, compatible Jest API, in-source testing
bytes.title: Jest to Vitest — Drop-in Migration with Vite-Native Speed
bytes.body:
Vitest runs your existing Jest suite with near-zero config changes. It uses the same `describe`, `it`, `expect`, `beforeEach`, and `vi` (a drop-in for `jest`) globals, so most test files migrate by just updating imports. The real win is speed: Vitest shares Vite's module graph, meaning HMR-aware test reruns skip re-transforming unchanged modules entirely — something Jest's `--watchAll` can't match.

The sharpest difference is in-source testing. You can colocate tests inside the source file itself using `if (import.meta.vitest)` guards, which tree-shakes to nothing in production builds. No separate `__tests__` directories needed for small utility functions.

For Jest users, the migration checklist is short: replace `jest.fn()` with `vi.fn()`, swap `jest.mock()` with `vi.mock()`, and set `globals: true` in `vitest.config.ts` to avoid adding explicit imports. Coverage still uses `@vitest/coverage-v8` or `istanbul`, same reporters. If your project already uses Vite, there's no reason to keep Jest.

bytes.topic: Playwright-specific — multi-browser, trace viewer, network interception
bytes.title: Jest + Playwright — Multi-Browser, Trace Viewer, and Network Interception
bytes.body:
jest-playwright-preset exposes `page`, `context`, and `browser` globals in every Jest test, letting you tap into Playwright's full API without ceremony.

For multi-browser coverage, set `browsers: ['chromium', 'firefox', 'webkit']` inside `testEnvironmentOptions` in `jest.config.js` — Jest runs each spec file once per browser, reporting failures per-browser label.

Network interception via `page.route()` is the cleanest way to stub flaky APIs in Jest integration tests. Call it before navigation; Playwright intercepts at the network layer — not the JS layer — so fetch, XHR, and service workers are all caught. Use `route.fulfill()` for mocked responses or `route.abort()` to simulate offline conditions.

For CI failures, wrap test bodies with `context.tracing.start({ screenshots: true, snapshots: true })` and `context.tracing.stop({ path: 'trace.zip' })`. Open the artifact with `npx playwright show-trace trace.zip` to replay every action with DOM snapshots and a full network waterfall — indispensable when a cross-browser assertion fails only on WebKit in CI.

Pair with `--testPathPattern` and `BROWSER=firefox` to isolate a single browser locally.

bytes.title: Jest Component Tests — Intercepting Network Requests Without cy.intercept
bytes.body:
Jest has no built-in network interception layer like cy.intercept, but pairing it with MSW (Mock Service Worker) gives you the same declarative request stubbing inside component tests. Set up a msw server in your Jest globalSetup, then use server.use() inside individual tests to override handlers per-scenario — without touching the component or mocking fetch directly.

Jest's jsdom environment renders the component tree, while MSW intercepts at the service worker level (via node integration in Jest), meaning your actual fetch/axios calls fire and get intercepted transparently. This catches bugs that jest.spyOn(global, 'fetch') misses: serialization issues, base URL mismatches, and header composition.

For component testing mode specifically, configure testEnvironment to jsdom in jest.config.ts and use @testing-library/react's render. Unlike Cypress component mode, Jest mounts in-process — no browser binary, sub-100ms per test. Combine with userEvent.setup() for realistic interaction sequences before asserting on the stubbed network response.

bytes.topic: Storybook — component documentation, interaction testing, story-driven development
bytes.title: Jest + Storybook — Reusing Stories as Jest Test Fixtures with composeStories
bytes.body: Storybook's `composeStories` utility lets Jest consume your `.stories` files directly as pre-configured render fixtures, eliminating the dual-maintenance problem of keeping test setup and story setup in sync. Import `composeStories` from `@storybook/react`, pass in the entire story module, and get back fully decorated components — with args, decorators, and loaders already applied. Render them in Jest via `@testing-library/react` exactly like any other component. This means a `Primary` story with mocked props becomes your Jest test's source of truth. When the story's args change, the Jest test automatically reflects it. For interaction testing, stories with `play` functions can be executed inside Jest using `@storybook/test-runner` or manually invoked via `composeStory` + `userEvent`. This pattern enforces story-first development: if you can't write a story for a component state, you can't test it either. It also makes CI meaningful — `jest` runs actual user-facing component configurations, not synthetic one-off test setups that diverge from what's documented.

bytes.topic: Code Coverage — what it tells you and what it doesn't, avoiding coverage theater
bytes.title: Jest Coverage — What the Numbers Hide and How to Avoid Coverage Theater
bytes.body:
Jest's `--coverage` flag reports four metrics: statements, branches, functions, and lines. A 90% score feels safe — but it can mask untested behavior entirely. Coverage measures execution, not correctness. A test that calls a function without asserting its return value inflates your numbers while catching nothing.

The most dangerous gap is branch coverage. Jest tracks `if/else`, ternaries, and `&&` short-circuits separately. A function with `if (user.isAdmin)` can hit 100% statement coverage with a single test, while the `false` branch — the one that blocks access — is never exercised.

Use `coverageThreshold` in `jest.config.ts` to enforce minimums per-directory, not globally. Global thresholds let low-coverage utility files drag down the average and hide gaps in critical domain logic. Pair coverage data with `--verbose` and mutation testing (via `jest-mutation-testing` or Stryker) to find tests that pass even when you break the implementation. Coverage is a floor, not a ceiling.
